steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure
steps.content: The blank appears within a single independent clause, positioned between the linking verb "is" and its complement "around 450 degrees Fahrenheit".

steps.title: Apply the punctuation rule
steps.content:
Left of blank: subject "The smoke point of corn oil." Right of blank: complement "around 450 degrees Fahrenheit." A linking verb connects directly to its complement — no punctuation belongs between them.
  • Choice A — em dash: "The smoke point of corn oil is— around 450 degrees Fahrenheit." — an em dash here implies a dramatic break or interruption; the complement is neither. ✗
  • Choice B — semicolon: "The smoke point of corn oil is; around 450 degrees Fahrenheit." — a semicolon must join two independent clauses; "around 450 degrees Fahrenheit" is a phrase, not a clause. ✗
  • Choice C — no punctuation: "The smoke point of corn oil is around 450 degrees Fahrenheit." — verb flows cleanly to complement. ✓
  • Choice D — comma: "The smoke point of corn oil is, around 450 degrees Fahrenheit." — a comma between linking verb and complement violates standard grammar. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
